title: Add coupon discount support to charge endpoint
summary: The panel landed on real problems: the silently-swallowed coupon error (c9), a discount_amount that always computes to zero (c10), a negative-charge path with no floor (c23), and PCI card data logged in plaintext (c22) are the must-fix core. Around those sit a consistent set of should-level cleanups: unvalidated couponCode (c3), debug logging, `as any` on the response, the doStuff name, lazy variable names, loose equality, the over-built strategy pattern, dead commented code, and missing tests. I collapsed the heavy duplication — the two console.log statements were each flagged by 3-4 personas under perf/standards/security, so I merged them (c6/c12→c22 for the card-data log, c2/c7/c15→c24 for the unknown-coupon log); the empty catch was raised three times (c4/c26→c9); the `any` response cast three times (c5/c25→c20); and doStuff naming twice (c13→c16). I dropped c8 (O(n) factory) as speculative — six coupons will never be a perf issue. I downgraded c11 (snake_case response fields) from must to should: it's a real consistency nit, not a blocker.

findings.title: Over-engineered lookup table as strategy pattern
findings.message: This is six hardcoded coupon codes wrapped in abstract class + two strategy subclasses + a factory. The pattern adds three exports and zero capability—you're not solving a problem, you're anticipating one that doesn't exist yet (YAGNI). Use a data structure instead: `const COUPONS: Record<string, { type: 'percent'|'fixed'; value: number }> = { WELCOME10: { type: 'percent', value: 10 }, ... }` and one `applyCoupon` function. When you have three real coupon *variants* with different behavior, reach for the pattern—then you'll know why.

findings.title: Delete commented-out code
findings.message: Commented-out branches rot and confuse future readers—was this a plan, a rollback, or dead code? Git preserves history. Delete it; if the feature is genuinely pending, add a task or TODO at the method level instead of embalming dead code.

findings.title: Unvalidated couponCode pulled from request body
findings.message: `const couponCode = theData.couponCode;` extracts the field from the body without schema validation. The field likely isn't in `chargeSchema`, so it reaches business logic untyped and untrusted. Add `couponCode: z.string().optional()` (or similar) to the schema so it's validated and typed before use.

findings.title: Silently swallowed error in coupon application
findings.message: The try-catch block around `doStuff()` catches exceptions but does nothing — it has only a TODO comment. This masks any error in coupon logic (bad code, bad state, etc.) and lets the request continue as if nothing happened. If coupon application fails, the error is invisible. Either handle the error meaningfully (log + alert, or reject the charge) or rethrow it to let the outer error handler deal with it. As written, a broken coupon service looks like success.

findings.title: Discount amount calculation is always zero
findings.message: After the try-catch, both `payload.amount` and `finalAmount` are equal (you set `payload.amount = finalAmount` on line 23). Then on line 31, you compute `response.discount_amount = payload.amount - finalAmount`, which is always 0. This defeats the entire feature. You need to save the *original* amount before applying the discount: `const originalAmount = payload.amount;` then `response.discount_amount = originalAmount - finalAmount;`

findings.title: Response field casing mixes conventions (snake_case breaks API contract)
findings.message: The response includes `discount_amount` and `coupon_code` in snake_case, but the rest of the codebase uses camelCase (see existing response fields like `customerId`, `currency`). This breaks the API contract for clients expecting consistent casing. Change to `discountAmount` and `couponCode`. A casing mismatch means clients consuming this endpoint have to special-case your response.

findings.title: Variable name 'theData' is uninformative
findings.message: `theData` is generic and gives no hint about what it contains. You're extracting the request body. Name it for what it is: `const chargeRequest = req.body;` or `const chargePayload = req.body;`. Then later `const couponCode = chargeRequest.couponCode` reads like actual code instead of a placeholder.

findings.title: Function name 'doStuff' is meaningless
findings.message: The exported function is called `doStuff`, which conveys no information about its purpose. Rename it to `calculateDiscountedAmount` or `applyDiscount`. A meaningful name makes the intent obvious; `doStuff` forces readers to study the body.

findings.title: Variable names 'tmp' and 'result2' are uninformative
findings.message: `var tmp = CouponHandlerFactory.build(couponCode)` and later `let result2 = tmp.apply(amount)` use lazy, unhelpful names. `tmp` means the author gave up; `result2` implies someone already used `result`. Use descriptive names: `const strategy = CouponHandlerFactory.build(couponCode)` and `const discountedAmount = strategy.apply(amount)`. Names are the cheapest documentation.

findings.title: Variable declaration uses 'var' instead of 'const'
findings.message: `var x = amount * (1 - this.p);` should be `const x = ...`. The codebase uses `const`/`let` for a reason (block scope, immutability, no hoisting confusion). Additionally, `x` is a meaningless name for a temporary calculation — use `const discounted = ...` or `const withDiscount = ...`

findings.title: Loose equality checks (== instead of ===)
findings.message: Lines 34, 37, 40, 43: `if (code == 'WELCOME10')` and similar use loose equality. Use strict equality: `if (code === 'WELCOME10')`. Also line 58: `if (tmp == null)` should be `if (tmp === null)`. The project likely enforces `eqeqeq` for good reason — loose equality leads to silent type coercion bugs.

findings.title: Type cast to 'any' circumvents type safety
findings.message: `const response: any = transaction;` bypasses TypeScript's type checking. You're using `any` just so you can add `discount_amount` and `coupon_code` fields. Instead, either extend the transaction type, create a new response type, or build a new object: `const response = { ...transaction, discountAmount: ..., couponCode: ... }`. Avoid `any`; it hides mistakes.

findings.title: Missing unit tests for coupon logic and edge cases
findings.message: The PR description marks unit tests for coupon math as 'TODO follow-up'. Money and discount code is high-value branching logic that should not ship untested. Test at minimum: valid coupons apply correct amounts, invalid coupons are rejected gracefully, edge cases (0 amount, amounts less than fixed discounts, rounding) are handled. The current code has no test coverage for this core feature.

findings.title: PCI-DSS violation: card data logged to console
findings.message: console.log('DEBUG charge body:', JSON.stringify(theData)) logs the full request body including card.number, card.cvc, and other sensitive fields. PCI-DSS forbids plaintext card data in logs. The codebase provides pino with redaction rules specifically to prevent this; bypassing it with console.log defeats the control and exposes PAN/CVC to stdout/log aggregators. Remove all DEBUG console statements before merge. If needed for troubleshooting, use the pino logger with field-level redaction: `logger.debug({ transactionId: payload.id }, 'charge processing')`.

findings.title: Missing validation: discount can result in negative charge
findings.message: Neither PercentageCouponStrategy nor FixedCouponStrategy validate that the final amount is non-negative. If a user applies TENOFF ($10, 1000 cents) to a $5 charge (500 cents), the result is -500 cents. This is passed unchecked to the payment gateway in paymentsController.ts:31. An attacker could use overlapping or large fixed-discount coupons on small-amount charges to trigger negative charges or unexpected refunds. Validate in both strategy.apply() that the result is >= 0, and reject it with an error if the discount exceeds the charge amount.

findings.title: Debug logging of user input (coupon codes)
findings.message: console.log('DEBUG: unknown coupon code:', couponCode) logs user-supplied input. While less severe than card data, this is a second instance of debug code that shouldn't ship. Remove it. If you need to track invalid coupon attempts for fraud detection, use the pino logger and limit what you log: `logger.warn({ couponCode: '***REDACTED***' }, 'invalid coupon attempt')` to avoid leaking patterns.
